text: The time-to-exhaustion during high-intensity exercise increases as a hyperbolic function of decreasing power or velocity, described by t = W'/(P - CP) where CP is critical power and W' is the curvature constant.
Critical power (CP) represents the highest sustainable rate of oxidative ATP production (VO2) that can be maintained without continuously depleting the finite energy store W'.
The W' parameter represents a finite energy store composed in part of anaerobic energy sources, expressed in kilojoules, and exhaustion occurs when W' is fully depleted.
Exercise performed above critical power will always result in exhaustion in a predictable time determined by the magnitude of W' and the degree to which power exceeds CP.
The critical power concept and the hyperbolic power-duration relationship are highly conserved across diverse species and different modes of exercise.
Critical power serves as a boundary between sustainable and unsustainable exercise intensity domains, making it a key threshold for prescribing training intensity and predicting exercise tolerance.
